senses.definition: Socks or other knitted items that have a pattern of colored diamonds on a plain background.
examples: He pulled up his bright argyles before putting on his shoes.
The golfer was known for wearing colorful argyles on the course.
She bought a pack of warm wool argyles for the winter.

senses.definition: A knitted pattern of colored diamonds on a plain background, used especially on socks and sweaters; also, a sock with this pattern.
examples: He wore an argyll vest over a white shirt.
Argyll socks were part of the school uniform.
The sweater has a traditional argyll across the front.

senses.definition: Socks or other knitted items that have the argyle or argyll diamond pattern.
examples: He packed three pairs of argylls for the trip.
Those argylls will match your brown shoes perfectly.
The shop sells only high-quality wool argylls.

senses.definition: A genus of butterflies, often called fritillaries, found especially in temperate regions.
examples: Several species of Argynnis are common in European meadows.
The researcher photographed an Argynnis feeding on wildflowers.
Argynnis butterflies are known for their orange and black markings.

senses.definition: Relating to or resembling plants of the daisy genus Argyranthemum.
examples: The garden featured several argyranthemous shrubs along the path.
Argyranthemous forms are popular in coastal landscapes.
They selected argyranthemous varieties for their long flowering season.

senses.definition: Having flowers that resemble those of the daisy genus Argyranthemum.
examples: The argyranthous blossoms brightened the rocky hillside.
Several argyranthous species tolerate dry, salty winds.
Gardeners often plant argyranthous perennials for summer color.

senses.definition: Elite infantry soldiers in the army of Alexander the Great and his successors, known for the silver-plated shields they carried.
examples: The Argyaspides were feared veterans in Alexander's campaigns.
Historians debate the exact number of Argyaspides at each battle.
The Argyaspides took their name from the silver shields they bore.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the skin and other body parts turn blue-gray after long exposure to silver or silver compounds.
examples: Long-term misuse of silver-based medicines can cause argyria.
The patient showed signs of generalized argyria after years of occupational exposure.
Argyria is usually permanent, even after stopping silver intake.

senses.definition: Plural of argyria; cases or types of the condition where the skin turns blue-gray due to silver exposure.
examples: Industrial hygienists documented several argyrias among workers.
The article compared localized and generalized argyrias.
Historical reports of argyrias often involved silver-based treatments.

senses.definition: Relating to silver or to the medical condition argyria.
examples: The team studied argyric pigmentation in exposed workers.
Argyric changes in the skin can be difficult to treat.
They examined the argyric effects of long-term silver therapy.

senses.definition: A silver mineral consisting mainly of silver sulfide, mined as an ore of silver.
examples: The mine produces argyrite along with other silver ores.
Argyrite often occurs in low-temperature hydrothermal veins.
They identified the black crystals as argyrite under the microscope.

senses.definition: Plural of argyrite; samples or pieces of the silver mineral silver sulfide.
examples: The collection includes several argyrites from South American mines.
Under polarized light, the argyrites showed distinct reflections.
They compared argyrites from different geological settings.

senses.definition: Having a head that appears silvery or shining like silver.
examples: The fish is argyrocephalous, with a bright metallic head.
Several argyrocephalous species live in deep ocean waters.
The argyrocephalous appearance helps the animal blend with the light above.

senses.definition: A rare silver mineral that contains silver, germanium, and sulfur, found in some ore deposits.
examples: Argyrodite was first discovered in a mine in Germany.
The sample contained both argyrodite and other silver minerals.
Argyrodite is an important source of germanium in some deposits.

senses.definition: Plural of argyrodite; pieces or occurrences of the silver-germanium mineral argyrodite.
examples: The museum displays several argyrodites from the original mine.
They analyzed argyrodites to determine their germanium content.
Argyrodites are rare compared with other silver minerals.

senses.definition: A medicine made from silver protein, once used as an antiseptic, especially in eye drops.
examples: Argyrol was widely used in the early twentieth century to prevent eye infections.
Doctors today rarely prescribe argyrol because safer drugs are available.
The old pharmacy bottle still contained traces of argyrol.

senses.definition: A genus of spiders, including the diving bell spider, which lives underwater in a web filled with air.
examples: Argyroneta aquatica is famous for building an underwater web.
The biologist studied the breathing system of Argyroneta.
Argyroneta spiders carry air bubbles to their silk nests.

senses.definition: A genus of small deep-sea fishes, sometimes called hatchetfishes, with thin bodies and light-producing organs.
examples: Species of Argyropelecus live in the open ocean at great depths.
The lantern-like organs of Argyropelecus help it hide from predators.
Argyropelecus is studied as a model for deep-sea adaptation.

senses.definition: An older medical term for argyria, the blue-gray discoloration of tissues from silver deposits.
examples: The textbook used the term argyrose for silver-related skin discoloration.
Argyrose was observed in patients treated with silver compounds.
Cases of argyrose decreased after the introduction of safer medicines.

senses.definition: Discoloration of tissues caused by deposits of silver, especially in the eye or skin.
examples: The doctor diagnosed argyrosis of the conjunctiva after long-term exposure to silver dust.
Argyrosis can affect the eyes more than the skin.
Occupational argyrosis is now rare because of better safety rules.

senses.definition: A genus of large marine fishes known as croakers or weakfish, important in commercial and sport fishing.
examples: Argyrosomus species are valued in coastal fisheries.
Researchers tagged several Argyrosomus to study migration.
Argyrosomus is common in temperate and subtropical waters.

senses.definition: A chemical compound that is a sugar combined with silver, studied mainly in chemistry.
examples: The chemist prepared argyrythrose in the laboratory.
Argyrythrose belongs to a group of silver-containing sugars.
The properties of argyrythrose were described in early chemical literature.

senses.definition: A tropical plant grown for its edible seeds, also called pigeon pea, widely used as a pulse in South Asia.
examples: Farmers in the region grow arhar as a major pulse crop.
Arhar requires warm weather and moderate rainfall.
Research has focused on improving arhar yields.

senses.definition: In Buddhism, a person who has reached enlightenment and is free from the cycle of rebirth.
examples: In some Buddhist traditions, the arhat is honored as an ideal.
The story tells how a monk became an arhat after many years of practice.
Statues of arhats line the walls of the temple hall.

senses.definition: Plural of arhat; enlightened persons in Buddhist tradition who have ended the cycle of rebirth.
examples: Paintings of the sixteen arhats decorate the monastery.
The text lists the names of famous arhats.
In the story, arhats protect the Dharma with their powers.

senses.definition: The state or quality of being an arhat in Buddhist teaching.
examples: He devoted his life to the pursuit of arhatship.
Arhatship is sometimes described as the highest goal in certain schools.
The text explains the qualities that define arhatship.

senses.definition: Plural of arhatship; instances or types of the state of being an arhat.
examples: The text discusses different arhatships in various Buddhist traditions.
Stories of arhatships were collected over many centuries.
Monks debated the nature of arhatships and bodhisattvahood.

senses.definition: An older spelling of Arhuaco, the name of an Indigenous people and their language in northern Colombia.
examples: Ethnographers once used the term Arhauaco in their reports.
The museum labels still show the older form Arhauaco.
Modern writers usually prefer the spelling Arhuaco instead of Arhauaco.

senses.definition: A condition where the heart does not beat with a normal regular rhythm.
examples: The patient was admitted with a severe arrhythmia.
Stress can sometimes trigger an arrhythmia.
Doctors monitor patients with arrhythmia carefully.

senses.definition: Plural of arrhythmia; different types or cases of irregular heartbeats.
examples: Certain arrhythmias can be life-threatening.
The study looked at common arrhythmias in older adults.
Doctors classify arrhythmias by their effect on heart rate.

senses.definition: Not having a regular rhythm, especially referring to the heartbeat.
examples: The electrocardiogram showed an arhythmic pattern.
Her pulse felt arhythmic during the examination.
Arhythmic heart activity may require urgent treatment.

senses.definition: Lacking a regular rhythm; arhythmic.
examples: The dancer's steps became arhythmical as she grew tired.
Arhythmical breathing can be a sign of distress.
The poem uses arhythmical lines to create tension.

senses.definition: In a way that does not follow a regular rhythm.
examples: His heart was beating arhythmically after the shock.
The lights flashed arhythmically on the damaged panel.
She breathed arhythmically as the pain increased.

senses.definition: A solo song with instrumental music in an opera or similar work.
examples: The soprano sang a famous aria from the opera.
His favorite aria always moves the audience to tears.
She practiced the aria for weeks before the performance.

senses.definition: In Greek mythology, the daughter of King Minos who helped Theseus escape the Labyrinth.
examples: Ariadne gave Theseus a thread to find his way out.
The painting shows Ariadne sleeping on the shore.
Teachers often use the story of Ariadne to explain mythic symbols.

senses.definition: Relating to the early Christian teaching of Arius, who argued that Christ was created and not equal to God the Father.
examples: The council condemned the Arian position as heresy.
Several bishops in the region supported Arian ideas.
Arian theology influenced early church debates.

senses.definition: A follower of the Christian teacher Arius or of his beliefs.
examples: The Arians were powerful for a time in parts of Europe.
As an Arian, he rejected the Nicene Creed.
Several Gothic kings were Arians.

senses.definition: A female given name.
examples: Ariana arrived early for the meeting.
They named their daughter Ariana.
Ariana plays the violin beautifully.

senses.definition: The set of Christian beliefs taught by Arius, especially that Christ was created by and is not equal to God the Father.
examples: Arianism was declared heretical at the Council of Nicaea.
The history course covered the rise and fall of Arianism.
Arianism challenged the developing doctrine of the Trinity.

senses.definition: A person who supports or believes in Arianism.
examples: The bishop was accused of being an Arianist.
Arianists were active in several regions of the empire.
The text defends the views of early Arianists.

senses.definition: Relating to supporters or beliefs of Arianism.
examples: The Arianist movement gained political support.
Arianist writings were widely copied.
The council rejected Arianist proposals.

senses.definition: Having qualities or ideas similar to Arian beliefs.
examples: Some passages in the text sound arianistic.
Critics labeled his views as arianistic.
The sermon contained arianistic elements.

senses.definition: Another form of arianistic; showing the character of Arian beliefs.
examples: The council condemned the arianistical opinions of some clergy.
His arianistical stance caused controversy.
The letter attacks what it calls arianistical errors.

senses.definition: Plural of Arianist; people who believe in or support Arianism.
examples: Arianists held several important church positions.
The emperor at one time favored the Arianists.
Councils were called to settle disputes between Arianists and their opponents.

senses.definition: To make something follow Arian beliefs or to interpret it in an Arian way.
examples: The author tried to Arianize certain traditional doctrines.
His opponents accused him of attempting to Arianize the church.
The text was Arianized in later copies.

senses.definition: A person who tries to spread or introduce Arian beliefs.
examples: He was described as an arianizer of the local clergy.
The pamphlet called the bishop a secret arianizer.
Arianizers influenced many communities during that period.

senses.definition: In Welsh mythology, a goddess associated with the moon and stars, whose name means 'silver wheel'.
examples: Arianrhod appears in several tales in the Mabinogion.
The festival honored Arianrhod as a lunar goddess.
Modern writers sometimes reinterpret Arianrhod as a symbol of female power.

senses.definition: The basic unit of money used in Madagascar.
examples: They exchanged dollars for ariary at the airport.
The price was ten thousand ariary.
He checked the exchange rate for the ariary.

senses.definition: Plural of aria; solo songs in an opera or similar work.
examples: The concert featured several famous arias.
She enjoys listening to Puccini arias.
The singer prepared three arias for the audition.

senses.definition: A complex plant carbohydrate related to arabinose, found in some gums and plant materials.
examples: The chemist isolated aribin from the plant extract.
Aribin is one of several polysaccharides present in the gum.
They measured the aribin content of the sample.

senses.definition: A chemical substance derived from aribin or related plant sugars.
examples: Researchers tested the solubility of aribine.
Aribine was obtained as a white powder after purification.
The team compared aribine with other plant-derived compounds.

senses.definition: Plural of ariboflavinosis; conditions caused by a lack of riboflavin (vitamin B2).
examples: Public health workers reported cases of ariboflavinoses in the region.
Ariboflavinoses often occur together with other nutrient deficiencies.
The study described the symptoms of several ariboflavinoses.
